item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact this was your first article is mindblowing. Fantastic work.
Hey, +200, everyone! Never expected we'd get here! \o/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this probably sounds a bit weird but, when i first read this article it didn't have the warning at the top and after reading it again with that warning in mind it was very difficult. i guess it never dawned on me that rainer was being gaslit so heavily and thought that the procedures being done on him were helping the foundation understand his powers but really it was selfish and cruel. this hit me hard because i've had many psychologists and even seen my fair share of abusive doctors, and i nearly burst into tears reading what they did to him now understanding it all. of course this isn't the articles fault, im still going to keep my +1 that i gave it when i first read it, but im probably not going to sleep well tonight. i relate with rainer a lot, i get lots of medical screenings, i want to help people as much as i can and im overly attached to my mom. i just want to give him a hug.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a unique interpretation. I'd be lying if I said I didn't have a similar thought while watching Cimmerian's latest video.

+ show block
– hide block

lolfoundation features prominently in that the researchers jump at the opportunity to exploit an anomaly

The exploitation of benign anomalies is not a strictly Series I idea. It's used sparingly, but if an anomaly is literally a spontaneous mass generator, it would not be out of character for the modern Foundation to use it.

My gut feels that the anomaly really is a proxy for the author ie "wouldn't it be cool if the foundation was real and I had these powers then this adventure might happen"

That's… very incorrect. Rainer is a straight dude who's trusting of hierarchical structures and government entities. I'm a transgirl anarcho-communist, although I am admittedly a teenager like Rainer. I'm also not a masochist, and my fantasy isn't to be tortured by the Foundation. If anything, I think I'd want to be a thaumaturgist…

Dr. [REDACTED] spent █████ to [DATA EXPUNGED]

Redactions and expungements are actually appropriate, given the story at hand. People complain about Series I redactions because SCP-096 performs [DATA EXPUNGED] on his victims; why is that data expunged? No one knows. Why is everything about 350-PROMETHEAN expunged? Massive retroactive ethics cover-up.

I come here, read this, and in isolation I'd upvote it and move on but I'm leaving this comment here b/c I need to leave a record of finding a complete double-standard in what I'm told the writing standards are and what we have here.

I'm gonna let you in on a little secret that only super successful writers know.1 You can write whatever you want. You can write a teenage superhero. You can write a crazy monster character. You can expunge as much data as you want.

The issue is that those are problem areas for new writers. It's hard to write superheroes and monsters and expungements. But we don't want to tell new writers that, because then they won't listen; they'll bank on being the exception and with a multitude of popular Series I articles sharing the tropes of their article, they'll go for it.

Of course, you can write anything here. You just need the right skill and help from other members. No serious author would refuse to enjoy an article on the basis of a trope; when you hear stuff like "we don't do monster entries anymore", it's shorthand for "it takes a lot of skill to write monster entries".

From now on, whenever I am reading potential submissions on #thecritters, if I come across lolfoundationy stuff I'm going to point straight at this article for proof positive that you indeed, CAN still do it.

Please do not do this. It is perfectly valid to use my article as an example of tropes used well if you are arguing with someone who genuinely thinks said tropes cannot be used well. The majority of critters understand the nuance, however. They have reviewed a multitude of articles and many have extrawiki experience with Literature education; they're not idiots. They just naturally feel a little awkward telling excitable new authors that they don't have the skill to do a certain trope.

– hide block

That's a thing. Best of luck with your quest.

Footnotes
1. Like me, with only 10 pages up on the site. Very successful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSIGH. I am extremely conflicted about this article, for reasons entirely unrelated to the article itself.

  1. lolfoundation features prominently in that the researchers jump at the opportunity to exploit an anomaly
  2. My gut feels that the anomaly really is a proxy for the author ie "wouldn't it be cool if the foundation was real and I had these powers then this adventure might happen"
  3. Dr. [REDACTED] spent █████ to [DATA EXPUNGED]

These are all the things I loved about series 1 articles, and still do love. However, I've been on the forums and on the IRC chat and hear the review staff and senior members all bleat about how "the writing standards for series 5 are higher" and "we don't do this or that anymore." Some of those who I see upvoting this very article.

I've been advancing one by one since 4000, reading hubs, crosslinks, and voting the entire way. I come here, read this, and in isolation I'd upvote it and move on but I'm leaving this comment here b/c I need to leave a record of finding a complete double-standard in what I'm told the writing standards are and what we have here.

From now on, whenever I am reading potential submissions on #thecritters, if I come across lolfoundationy stuff I'm going to point straight at this article for proof positive that you indeed, CAN still do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Resurrection canon has been on my radar for a while, but I've never sat down and read anything from it. I'm hesitant to use Rainer that way, given that he'll be a main character in my Site-17 canon.

We'll see once I read Resurrection and see if his usage would be appropriate, or even possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would it actually be possible to implement Rainer into the Resurrection Storyline? After all, Alpha-9 is in the business of using humanoid anomalies and normal Mtf troopers to carry out the Foundation's orders. Not to mention, Alpha-9 has some degree of influence within the Ethics Committee (Cimmerian) and one of the 05s ( 10 I believe, or whichever unknown one is backing them). His story is slightly similar to 105, in which they both have powers they never really wanted or know how to fully use, but want to help others?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My apologies that you didn't enjoy the article. Coincidentally, I've returned to it (again) to clean it up with more writing experience under my belt, so your critique is just what I needed. I'll continue to work on the article with what you said in mind.

That being said, a few things...
– hide block
  • Two experiment logs from Addendum.4051.3 have been excised as per your suggestion. Further efforts to shorten the article without sacrificing characterization will continue.
  • When I wrote the Beast tale, I was more concerned with character arcs and the story itself than believability. I'd personally be willing to suspend my disbelief if those two things were satisfied. Whether or not they were satisfied is another issue.
  • Prior to the first mass revision of this article, Rainer was characterized as much more juvenile in the exploration log and was actively upset and panicky throughout. During the revision, I found that characterization to be distasteful and annoying. Realistically, yes, Rainer would be extremely distressed at the situation, but I'm unsure what the reader would gain from that.
  • Rainer's activities in the log extend to summoning an incendiary device, shooting fire at spiders with significant help from his teammates, and outrunning someone with one arm who has suffered significant blood loss. It's safe to say he's not too unrealistically strong.
  • Say they're anomalous painkillers. I'm unsure how having Morgan be unconscious would improve the story.
  • Morgan's being non-binary isn't pandering. I did not write Morgan with the hopes this tale would be championed by non-binary SCP fans as the pinnacle of representation.
  • Agreed, Rainer's explanation of his newfound ability is poorly executed. I'll alter it in the future.
  • Rainer's "kill all move" is the natural escalation of his newfound ability, executed as a result of his mental instability.
  • It's possible your confusion over the ending is the result of a misreading of the article. Rainer is essentially being gaslit into thinking the abuse he suffers at the hands of Edwards is actually just him helping the Foundation. His contentment in the end is his acceptance of that lie.

Again, sorry you don't like it. Hope you can return to it at some point and find it suits your tastes more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just read SCP-4051 and the accompanying tale ''The Beast Beneath The Library' in one go, and because my criticism encompasses both of those works, I thought I'd leave them here on the main SCP page for convenience.

To just come out with it from the get-go: SCP-4051 gets a no-vote from me, the tale gets a downvote. I initially started out quite liking the SCP,
for a variety of reasons.
for a variety of reasons:
  1. There is some nice foreshadowing at the beginning on 4051 getting reclassified to Keter and some really fucky things going on with the Ethics Committee, so obviously some morally grey things happened.
  2. I think the personification of Rainer is quite strong at the beginning, where he realistically has quite an aversion to the Foundation and just wants to go home to his mom, but over time learns he can become useful (tieing in with his vigilantism). He evolves into what I would consider a realistic and fleshed out character just before Addendum 6.
  3. The escalation from the level 3/4051 classification onwards is also quite good. The process of a doctor going to great lengths, trying to understand how 4051 works and bolstering its ability to do the dirty work for the Foundation (at least, that's how I interpreted it) was neat. However, it really was on the long side. When I saw how many logs/experiments there were, I cringed and sighed. It's hard to say what I would cut, but I would probably prune a few experiment logs that just end up saying '350-Promethean didn't end up working again'.

However, with all that build-up to 350-Promethean and 350-Janus (which I guess is the Foundation's attempt at making an universal constructor/thing-maker/'summon-things-from-alternate-reality-just-like-4051 Mark I (tm)'?), Addendum 6 just straight up fails to deliver. What we get is another cliché instance of the Foundation using a skip in a MTF in a ridiculously irresponsible way, just like Able and MTF Omega-7. 4051 being released to help around Site-17 with menial tasks was one thing, but to let him run around with a MTF on a highly dangerous mission for no other reason than to 'give him a test run' is, in my opinion, dumb.

Besides the premise of Addendum 6 being poor, :

I have some other critique that I think weighs heavily on it.
I have some other critique that I think weighs heavily on it:
  1. Rainer's behavior is wholly unbelievable and incompatible with his characterization in the interview/experiment logs preceding Addendum 6. His prior experience with using his powers, according to interview 1, involved hitting carjackers over the head with an aluminum baseball bat. Now, he's a calm and collected, efficient member of the MTF who doesn't shit his pants or starts screaming the instant person-sized spiders with human heads start attacking him, or when he's surrounded by ghosts and other entities that warp reality. He doesn't fucking BLINK when he has to amputate someone's arm.
  2. On top of Rainer's unbelievable behavior, I believe Addendum 6 is just straight up poorly written, for two reasons:
    1. The dialogue is not good. Up until the portal opens up, besides Rainer's behavior, everything is mostly fine. It's a good mix of professionalism you would expect from elite MTF members and believable human behavior the moment giant spiders come running at you, or break your leg, in the case of Jayla. However, after this, things take a turn for the worse.
      1. Regardless of how many painkillers Morgan would take, they would not make expositional dialogue with Rainer 30 seconds after having their arm cut off at the shoulder.
      2. The casual mention of them being non-binary feels cheap and, honestly, borderline pandering.
      3. The line where Rainer explains how he manifested 'the exact opposite of it' feels incredibly anime-y. It was purely there to explain to us what the hell he just did.
    2. It just straight up turns Deus Ex Machina/Dragonball-Z at the end. I have no clue what's going on with the ghost, the 'self-doubt' and how Rainer pulls a 'kill-all' move straight out of his ass.

After the brunt of Addendum 6 left me very confused and moderately annoyed because of the wasted potential, I got even more confused because of the consequences of Addendum 6 don't seem to make any sense either. So Rainer has gained the ability to delete things at will by creating their inverse (however that works), but why does that warrant a reclassification to Keter? He has shown no evil intentions or activate hostility. We have contained other reality benders that are classified as Euclid. Also, why is he so content with being told he's never going to be allowed to help ever again? Why does he sit down in a corner and just smile? This also does not line up with his previous characterization of 'someone who needs to be feel like they're helping, or else they become depressed'.

So overall, this had quite a terrific start that, in my view, crashed and burned with Addendum 6. From that point on, there was poor dialogue, unsatisfying characterization and a poor overall wrap-up that doesn't do your character justice. It's obviously your skip to write, but what I would've done is let procedure 350-Promethean/Janus go wrong and, as a consequence of that, let 4051 go mad/berserk/mad, turning him actively hostile to the Foundation, thus telling the cautionary tale to the reader of how pushing a humanoid SCP can make things go bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, thanks for pointing that out! Don’t know how that fell through, I’ve revised this SCP almost three times so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait… the nets measure 3 m2 by 2 m2?

Three square meters by two square meters?

Unless the nets are hyperplanes or that's a fancy way of saying "nine meters by four meters," those measurements make no sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a really cool read, usually long articles would bore me but this one kept me interested with it's characterization and the pacing of the texts. The cold-hearted nature of the Foundation mixed with Jane's therapy sessions (especially her letter to Graham) packed a punch in my heart.

item.title: Hello!
item.content:encoded: An author and member of the community who continues to show constant dedication to the fandom and contributes immensely to this incredible community. (This is Amy/Jacob) I look forward to speaking to you more in the future, when time and our situations permit us to do so. I love you and your works, Nagiros! <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm happy you enjoyed the article, and I must apologize for replying so late. I've been dealing with some substantial personal problems as of late.

If you're looking for good humanoid SCPs, I've linked a few in the infobox of the article next to the rating module. You seem like you'd enjoy the third one most.

To answer your question, Rainer attempted to retaliate in some way while the procedure was being executed. That probably meant breaking the restraints or attempting to attack whoever was operating on him, which prompted Edwards to vocalize his hatred for Rainer because of how he screwed up Roswell, his brother.

Procedure 350-TITYOS, which will soon get a name-change during the rewrites, is a process in which Edwards surgically cuts open Rainer's arm and examines the nerves and muscle as Rainer manifests a portal, which naturally requires him to be conscious for the whole affair. Quite nasty, really.

Thanks again for the patient read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really enjoyed this. Some other lengthy articles were rage-inducing but I read this one in one go without attention skip.

I guess I craved for a story about humanoid SCP(tm) who would have to adapt to living in the foundation, I wanted a story how would they interact with security, researchers, psychologists.
I'm sure someone more versed in scp would throw a rock at me, naming a dozen similar articles I'm not aware of (cactus-man comes to mind, which is pretty short, or 1504 who didn't actually have to live on a Foundation site)

Off the records, author, would you like to hint us what 4051 did to dr Edwards and what Procedure 350-TITYOS entails?

In the end, I imagine dr Edwards and dr David were not the most ethical doctors if the Ethics Committee bulldozered them. And dr David now holds it as personal against Rainer!
You know scp-596? Check it out if you don't. I've been a little shook but not really surprised by SCP decisions there (you heard about femur breaker? get ready for the Lobotomizer!), but that article is very clinical and does not have a tale, which is p significant for humanizing a human SCP. I hope it will be later updated on what's up with Rainer.

My guess would be that Rainer manifested something that fucked dr Edward's psyche and the procedure… I'm at loss here. I can only guess it was physically/mentally painful if dr Edwards resorted to yelling and made Rainer lash out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [Deleted cause the site won't let me.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess it is true. Everything old is new again. +1, because it's well written and because I'm interested if history will repeat itself or things will go in a different direction this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because the string of characters is inherently nonsensical. Rainer may have interpreted that to mean "summon big metal characters", but he didn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It didn't exactly come off that way in the article, it seemed like they were speaking in grawlixes and gave off a Sunday Funnies vibe.

As for the last point, if they're just saying the names of these things to him, shouldn't he summon big metal parentheses and asterisks and stuff? Why does it produce some crazy mind bending cogito hazard instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm unsure how it's stupid. The idea of the test is to see how he'll react to nonsensical concepts, and a string of characters certainly holds no inherent meaning.

How do you pronounce it? Easy: "caret, percent symbol, open parenthesis, asterisk, closed parenthesis".

Yes, that is the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I have to downvote because of the whole ^%(*) thing.

- It sounds utterly stupid
-How would you pronounce it? (People "say" ^%(*) several times in the article)
-It's not a concept, it's random characters,how could he even imagine anything to summon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, thanks a ton! Happy you enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty well written for a first time file. I'd say that you might want to cut down on the length just a tad bit. Other than that, it's refreshing to see an SCP who just wants to help. It's definitely better than my first SCP for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the recovery seems fine to me. I'll be on the lookout for dry sections of the article, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I sort of forgot which parts but I do think you could do without the Recovery of the SCP. If not, shorten it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree then that it's better the way it is. I think making him hostile would be weird considering the rest of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks a ton. I'll implement your suggestions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The file evolved as I was writing, and I think the end is much higher quality than the beginning.

I'm going to hold you to that. I'll go through the rest later and see if I can say anything else servicable.

Response to 'Longer Response'

+ show block
– hide block

Yes. The line you quoted is what I was referring to. I do not think it gives the impression you think it does — 'trusting of authority figures' is something that comes across already at the start of the conversation.

Rather than revealing character, it felt like the focus with that line was to exposit/reveal a facet of the anomaly.

If the exchange is made to showcase his eagerness to be transparent/helpful (or showing his trust of personnel), then a more smooth response to "Not at the moment. Why do you ask?" should suffice. Instead of blurting out a detail about his power, he could respond by explaining his intentions in fully cooperating:

"Well, I mean, it's just that you guys seem to have a better handle on this stuff than I do. I thought maybe you can help me, I don't know, figure things out?"

That's terrible because I'm ass a dialogue, but I hope you get my drift.

I don't think that information — that each pocket universe is filled with like items — cannot be included at all in conversation, but that the current implementation feels unnatural.

I'm unsure how I'm supposed to avoid talking about it.

Easy. Refer to his number in the documentation, use his name to his face. They could inform him of his designation, sure, but the forced stuffiness of calling humanoid skips by their number as a rule, I feel, adds a cliche level of melodrama.

Eh, you mean the bomb, the gun, or the knife?

The gun. I completely forgot that he had a knife when I made that comment.

Thing is: the escape was clearly planned to some degree. That's evident in him manifesting improvised armor and explosives. This also comes at a time after which he's learnt of his ability to conjure up the impossible — only limited to what he could imagine.

Now, I'm going to sound like a horrible pendant here, but it's this line in particular that elicited an eyeroll:

SCP-4051 appears unable or unwilling to use lethal force against the personnel, attempting to inflict incapacitating but non-lethal injuries to legs, arms, and the torso.

Because it felt like I was being led by the hand and reassured of his goodness, but in a way that made it an Informed Trait. That is, we don't see his reluctance to do harm in his actions, but rather, by being explicitly told "it's oaky, he didn't do anything too awful".

To wrap back around to my comment about the extent of his ability/his planning: since he knows he can pretty much get anything from these portals, why not use something non-lethal to begin with? *Show* in his actions that he has no intent to kill, rather than *telling* with such an aside. I've no suggestions off the top of my head, besides something fantastical like, I dunno, lobbing globs of incapacitating goo? Or, something closer to reality: a gun that fires sticky foam?

And quick tangent, but I don't think they'd use tranqs against a humanoid - they could very well inadvertantly kill someone with an overdose like that. Tazers seem like a more sensible non-lethal detainment method.

I'm a bit new around here. Is it uncommon for authors to delete under-performing but positively-rated files to make something better?

You can either edit the page, or if you're going to make substantial changes, delete and repost when it's rewritten. I've done both, and I think deletion/repost is better for visibility — there's only so much one can do to bring eyes back to something they've already cast votes on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe not deletion, but altering them? Do certain sections feel dry?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure about it though. As it stands, it is pretty serviceable. I feel like if I did point out thise sections for removal, it would ruin the whoke thing.

This is just me after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I've gotten a few suggestions for how else to end it, namely having Rainer be openly hostile to containment, but nothing's really sat right with me other than the current ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is a bit long but I loved this one and was interested through out the whole thing. Rainer really felt like a teenager wanting to be a superhero and save the world. The final bit felt a little anticlimatic though. I'm not sure how else to end it but it just felt a little out of place to end it like that for me.

Still loved it though and I wanted to read more about him by the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the input. Quite frankly, I wasn't entirely happy with the elements you mentioned at the time of posting. The file evolved as I was writing, and I think the end is much higher quality than the beginning.

+ Longer Response
- Longer Response

When you talk about the exposition in the interview, are you referring to when SCP-4051 says the following?

SCP-4051: Sometimes when I use my power and reach into the other place, I can feel others of the same thing that I want to pull out. Like, it’s a space filled with that object.

The idea there was that this was a facet of 4051's character, that he was naturally trusting of authority. He had information that he thought might help, and he shared. Is there any way I can articulate that better?

Also, yes, the bit about not being a number. This was the part I was least satisfied with, but I kept it unchanged because I'm unsure how I'm supposed to avoid talking about it. A reaction like this is logical, if uninteresting. Do you have any pre-existing humanoid SCPs that deal with this issue better?

hand/finger restraints seem like a logical go-to.

Well, shit, ran this past twenty-odd people and that never occurred to any of us. Thanks.

He's so good and righteous as to not attempt to kill his captors, but attempts escape using a lethal weapon?

Eh, you mean the bomb, the gun, or the knife? Regardless, I tried to point out in the incident report that he'd only use the last two to inflict non-lethal injuries. Is there a way to articulate that better?

I lost interest by the conclusion of the Incident Log: seeing another 6 or so collapsibles worth of content I was already having multiple issues with didn't inspire me to tread on.

Entirely understandable, which is why your comment is so important. Thanks a ton for reading as far as you did.

And there's a number of "it's" mistakes that could stand correcting.

Thanks, I'll work on those. Give me a bit.

- Longer Response

I'm not entirely satisfied with this file as it is now, nor am I really happy with just having an article that doesn't get deleted. It's at +10 at the time of writing, and while that's fine and I'm happy so many people enjoyed it, I'd still like something better.

I'm a bit new around here. Is it uncommon for authors to delete under-performing but positively-rated files to make something better?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted by the end of the first interview at the sight of some poorly-handled exposition. It didn't feel like a natural development of the conversation, rather, it gave the sense of trying to justify how the SCPF learned of a specific trait detailled in the Description.

Beyond that, the whole illogical and frighteningly cliche "I am not a number" exchange is the type of theatrics I eternally hope dies off for good for how rote it is typically in execution. It adds a needless level of melodrama to humanoid containment, and makes so little sense to think they'd dehumanize a humanoid like that on one hand, but also offer it therapy on the other.

I also feel there's probably a more sensible way to contain its effects beyond hand-wringing over Object Class and sticking it in a stronger room - hand/finger restraints seem like a logical go-to. If he can't articulate in order to grab something, he's incapable of accessing dangerous contraband.

Characterization, too, is a touch wonky: He's so good and righteous as to not attempt to kill his captors, but attempts escape using a lethal weapon?

I lost interest by the conclusion of the Incident Log: seeing another 6 or so collapsibles worth of content I was already having multiple issues with didn't inspire me to tread on.

And there's a number of "it's" mistakes that could stand correcting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you point me to a few of those sections? It's probably too late to make any major deletions, but it would help me with my future writing, regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I don't really know. On one hand, you've got a great narrative and fleshed out some of your characters to make me care about them.

On the other, this feels a bit too long. There's some sections that could be removed and it would feel less weighty. And it's about a kid that has your stereotypical superpower. But then again, I'm just not used to reading about people with powers.

I don't know, it's just that it feels weird in a way that I can't put my finger on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ha, yeah, sorry. The story currently comes in at around 14k words, Tale included. I figured writing longer was the only way to make a file with as many moving parts as this work.

Was there anything you think it could benefit from?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honest to God. The sheer scope and scale of this… The magnitude of this article is pretty staggering considering its about a 17 year old kid with your what-have-you superpower.

I don't know whether I should upvote or downvote, so I'll just novote on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great. It’s well written; it is a very interesting concept; it also captures the dynamics between a childlike disposition and the cold, clinical tone of the Foundation - whether that was intentional or not doesnt matter - very well.

This is your first work, right? Mighty fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha, that'd help. This thing's been hell with spelling and grammar. I've gone over it maybe twenty times and run it past a few editors, and I still feel like I'm finding stuff to correct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better. I may make a separate post for any errors I may spot when I am done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gotcha. How's it look now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, I never made the connection to 076, and I hadn't read 1162. Sorry about that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was my main gripe with the concept too. The start of the Description reminded me of 076 which made me a bit iffy about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better actually. Currently I am reading it again. The article is starting to grow on me. A bit of advice, the recovery is one huge paragraph, so I recommend breaking it down to two. It looks like a slog to read through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great idea for an SCP, and it's written very well. I just wanted to point out that the bit about the portal reminds me too much of SCP-076 and SCP-1162. SCP-076-2 can summon a portal in which he grabs stuff (specifically bladed weapons) and SCP-1162 is a hole in the wall that you grab stuff you've lost out of. It sounds like a combination of these two. SCP-076-2's portal in which seemingly random items are pulled out of (SCP-1162). Other than that, I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've altered the table to fit with your suggestion. How's that look?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll try it out. Thanks for the help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looking at the code, you are using a different version of it. When in edit, there is an icon that allows automatic insert of code for tables. It is in the second row, fourth icon. You should have something like this:

+ show block
– hide block
header header
cell-content cell-content
cell-content cell-content
cell-content cell-content
cell-content cell-content
cell-content cell-content

The coding should look like this:

 ||~ header ||~ header || || cell-content || cell-content ||

Perhaps that may improve the display.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry about that! Do you know of a way to fix it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The table messes up the display on mobile which I am on currently.

item.title: Author Note
item.content:encoded:
This is my first file, and I'm happy you took the time to read it. Hope you enjoyed!

A special thanks to Reverend Fox for the extremely helpful critique, and my many friends, who I badgered into reading this.

Edit 3/7/19: Rewrites are done, everything's generally better now.
